coredata.dc:title: Light-up lipid droplets dynamic behaviors using rationally designed carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yachu
Guo, Jianhua
Xu, Hongmei
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Lipid droplets (LDs) are essential organelles used to store lipids and participate in cellular lipid metabolism. Imaging LDs is an intuitive approach to comprehend their biological functions. Herein, the LDs-targeted CDs (LD-CDs) featuring robust solvatochromic emission were elaborately designed by a Schiff base reaction using 1, 2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene, 3-dimethylaminophenol, and thiourea as precursors. The LD-CDs exhibited a remarkable sensitivity to polarity changes over a broad linear range (0.0205–0.3213), owing to its unique intramolecular charge transfer effect (ICT). Moreover, the favorable lipophilicity of LD-CDs endows it with the capability to light up LDs with high specificity. Due to their good lipophilicity and biocompatibility, the LD-CDs were able to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells and keep real-time track of LDs dynamic behaviors, including dissociation, migration, and fusion. Leveraging the multifunctionality of LD-CDs, we have also observed the dynamic changes of lysosomes and LDs during lipophagy. Additionally, the LD-CDs were employed to reveal the polarity change of LDs in living cells and zebrafish under oleic acid stimulation and to visualize lipid metabolism in zebrafish. We deem that this work will expand the applications of CDs in biological imaging and make further contributions to the field of LDs-associated metabolism and diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LD-CDs
Polarity
Lipid droplets
Dynamic imaging
Zebrafish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design strategy, preparation and characterization of the LD-CDs
Diagnosis of cancer cells by LDs-specific labeling
Visualization of the dynamic behavior of LDs
Monitoring the process of lipophagy between LDs and lysosomes
Monitoring LDs changes induced by oleic acid
Visualization of lipid metabolism in zebrafish

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of LD-CDs and their HR-TEM images (Top inset). (b) FTIR spectrum of LD-CDs. (c) XPS spectra of LD-CDs. (d) The UV–vis absorption and excitation-emission spectra of LD-CDs. (e–f) Normalized spectra and chromaticity color coordinates in the CIE diagram of the LD-CDs in various solvents. (g) Fluorescence indicator spectra of LD-CDs in various percentages of 1,4-dioxane/H2O mixtures; inset: photographs in various proportions of 1,4-dioxane/H2O mixtures under a 365 nm lamp. (h) Linear plot of fluorescence intensity versus Δf. (i) Absorption spectra of LD-CDs in n-octanol (CA), emission spectra of LD-CDs in water and CA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Organelle co-localization fluorescence imaging of the LD-CDs (40 μg/mL) and commercial organelle dyes: Nile Red (50 nM), Mito-tracker (300 nM), Lyso-tracker (300 nM), and Hoechst (1 μM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Tracking LDs movements in HeLa cells by LD-CDs: Bright-field images acquired at 0, 2, 4, and 6 min and the merged image. (b) The enlarged dynamic diagram of the white box from (a).

(a) CLSM images of HeLa cells cultured by LD-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Red under starvation conditions for different periods. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence images of (a–b) HeLa cells or zebrafish stimulated with OA (200 μM) for different times. (c–d) Change tendency in average fluorescence intensities of images a or b.
(a–d) Confocal microscopic images of zebrafish at different growth period treated with LD-CDs. (e) Average fluorescence intensity obtained from a-d.
Schematic representation of the synthetic route of LD-CDs and their use for tracking lipid droplets dynamics.

head.ce:title: Light-up lipid droplets dynamic behaviors using rationally designed carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanxi University of Electronic Science and Technology, Linfen, 041000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Shanxi University of Electronic Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanxi University of Electronic Science and Technology, Linfen 041000, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lipid droplets (LDs) are essential organelles used to store lipids and participate in cellular lipid metabolism. Imaging LDs is an intuitive approach to comprehend their biological functions. Herein, the LDs-targeted CDs (LD-CDs) featuring robust solvatochromic emission were elaborately designed by a Schiff base reaction using 1, 2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene, 3-dimethylaminophenol, and thiourea as precursors. The LD-CDs exhibited a remarkable sensitivity to polarity changes over a broad linear range (0.0205–0.3213), owing to its unique intramolecular charge transfer effect (ICT). Moreover, the favorable lipophilicity of LD-CDs endows it with the capability to light up LDs with high specificity. Due to their good lipophilicity and biocompatibility, the LD-CDs were able to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells and keep real-time track of LDs dynamic behaviors, including dissociation, migration, and fusion. Leveraging the multifunctionality of LD-CDs, we have also observed the dynamic changes of lysosomes and LDs during lipophagy. Additionally, the LD-CDs were employed to reveal the polarity change of LDs in living cells and zebrafish under oleic acid stimulation and to visualize lipid metabolism in zebrafish. We deem that this work will expand the applications of CDs in biological imaging and make further contributions to the field of LDs-associated metabolism and diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipid droplets (LDs) are extremely dynamic organelles that consist of a neutral lipid core and surrounding phospholipid monolayer membranes [1–3]. Since LDs are indispensably involved in a variety of cellular processes, including membrane transport, apoptosis, lipogenesis, and cargo exchange, they have been widely recognized for their role in regulating cellular lipid metabolism [4,5]. There is growing evidence that accumulation of LDs is associated with a range of metabolic diseases, such as fatty liver, atherosclerosis, cardiovascular disease, and so on [6]. This suggests that LDs could serve as a versatile biomarker. Moreover, cancer is characterized by changes in metabolic processes, which can result in an acidic tumor environment [7–9], consequently facilitating the synthesis and further accumulation of LDs. Given the unique role of LDs, real-time tracking of LDs-related dynamic behaviors is significant for revealing clinical diagnoses and biological activities.

Fluorescent probe technology has been extensively considered as an effective tool for versatile biomedical imaging because of the dominance of high spatiotemporal resolution, rapid response, excellent sensitivity, and in situ properties [10–12]. A variety of fluorescent probes based on small molecule dyes have been developed for imaging LDs [13–16], such as BODIPY, Nile-Red, flavonoids, coumarin, and so on. Nevertheless, some of these fluorescent molecules come with some serious limitations. For example, BODIPY 493/503 exhibits a small Stokes shift, which could lead to the severe “self-absorption” effect. Nile Red shows a poor selectivity for LDs imaging and may stain other hydrophobic cellular structures [17,18]. Additionally, these probes easily suffer from complex synthetic procedures and poor water solubility, restricting their applicability in biological research. Therefore, engineering new LDs probes with an easy preparation approach, a large Stokes shift, and excellent LD staining selectivity is urgently needed.

Carbon dots (CDs), with the merits of fascinating optical properties and high biocompatibility, are highly promising for precise imaging and real-time monitoring of intracellular LDs [19–22]. So far, several CDs have been successfully used for LDs labeling in living cells [23–27]. For instance, Gao et al. synthesized LDs-targeting CDs by a solvothermal synthesis route using oPD and thiourea, which could serve as a competent probe for high-fidelity imaging of LDs [25]. Wang et al. presented N, N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine-derived CDs with red emission for imaging LDs and realizing fluorescence diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma [26], which will provide a novel perspective for the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in LDs-related diseases. Besides, Liu et al. presented full-color CDs with blue, green, and red emissions to visualize the variability in lipid composition among LDs [27]. Although these CDs have realized LDs imaging, it is still rarely reported for monitoring the numerous dynamic processes of LDs and revealing polarity differences of LDs in vitro and in vivo. Particularly, growing evidence indicates that probes with ICT effects are highly sensitive to polarity, which could be employed to design polarity-sensitive LDs probes [28–31]. Fortunately, the critical characteristic for LDs-specific targeting is feasible for CDs. Altogether, it is highly desirable to design CDs with polarity-responsive features and outstanding LDs affinity for tracking their dynamic behaviors.

Herein, a polarity-sensitive fluorescence probe based on CDs with LDs-targeting capability (LD-CDs) was designed and synthesized by a Schiff base reaction of 1, 2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene, 3-dimethylaminophenol, and thiourea in ethanol (Scheme 1 ). The obtained LD-CDs possessed specific lipophilic groups (e.g., methyl and aromatic units), thereby enabling them to selectively light up LDs. Particularly, the LD-CDs exhibited low fluorescence in high-polarity media, which may be ascribed to the existence of the unique D-π-A structure and the ICT properties. Due to the specific-polarity sensitivity and proper hydrophobicity of LD-CDs, the multiple dynamic processes of LDs were initially detected, including the dissociation, migration, fusion, and lipophagy processes. Moreover, it was noteworthy that LD-CDs effectively facilitated the discrimination of cancer cells. Additionally, we demonstrated how the polarity of LDs changes in living cells, the different polarities of lipid-rich areas in zebrafish, and the metabolism of lipids in zebrafish by utilizing LD-CDs.

3-dimethylaminophenol (60 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (20 mL). The pH value of the solution was adjusted to 10 using sodium hydroxide (1 M). Next, 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene (25 mg), thiourea (10 mg) were added to the above solution and mixed uniformly under ultrasonication. The mixture was sealed into an autoclave (50 mL) and subjected to solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 6 h. Afterwards, the obtained solution was treated by evaporating the ethanol, dispersing it in deionized water, and then centrifuging (10000 rpm, 15 min). The primary purified product was subsequently purified through a dialysis membrane (100 Da) for 24 h and filtration (0.22 μm membrane). Lastly, LD-CDs were obtained as brown solid powder by lyophilization.

The powder of LD-CDs was dissolved in ethanol to prepare 3 mg/mL stock solutions. Test solutions were obtained by adding 24 μL of LD-CDs solution (3 mg/mL) into the corresponding solvent (organic solvents, water, and different proportions of 1,4-dioxane and water) at an ultimate concentration of 36 μg/mL. The resulting mixed solution was thoroughly shaken and then measured at an excitation of 420 nm.

HeLa cells, A-549 cells, HepG2 cells (cancer cells), and HL-7702 cells (normal cells) were cultured in DMEM containing 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 and 95 % air. HeLa cells were chosen as model cells to assess the possibility of the obtained LD-CDs for cellular imaging. HeLa cells were seeded in 25-mm glass-bottom dishes for 24 h. In LDs imaging research involving cancer and normal cells, the cells were incubated with LD-CDs (40 μg/mL) for 2 min before being utilized for imaging. Finally, all observations were conducted using a confocal fluorescence microscope (Zeiss LSM 880).

In the colocalization experiments: HeLa cells were cultured with Nile Red (50 nM) for 20 min, and then treated with LD-CDs for an additional 2 min. Co-staining tests utilizing commercial organelle probes, including Lyso Tracker Red, MitoTracker Deep Red, and Hoechst, were performed using a method analogous to the aforementioned methodology. LD-CDs: Ex/Em = 488 nm/520−580 nm; Nile Red: Ex/Em = 561 nm/600–670 nm; Lyso Tracker Red: Ex/Em = 633/650−750 nm; Mito Tracker Deep Red: Ex/Em = 633/650−750 nm; Hoechst 33342 Ex/Em = 405/410–470 nm.

In the cell imaging experiment stimulated by Oleic Acid: HeLa cells were first incubated with LD-CDs for 2 min, followed by culture with oleic acid (200 μM) for varying durations (0–2 h). Fluorescence images were acquired using a confocal microscope.

Zebrafish maintenance was performed in agreement with the protocol outlined in earlier research [32]. Zebrafish embryos at various developmental stages (12, 24, 48, and 72 hpf) were incubated with methylene blue-rich deionized water. Firstly, the zebrafish embryos or larvae were incubated with LD-CDs for 10 min. The stained embryos or larvae were transferred to a new dish after being rinsed with PBS buffer three times. Fluorescence images at different time points were acquired on Zeiss LSM880. During the imaging procedure, the zebrafish larvae were anesthetized by 0.003 % MS-222.

In the zebrafish imaging experiment stimulated by Oleic Acid: The larvae were exposed to LD-CDs for 10 min, then cultured with oleic acid (200 μM) for real-time recording of 2 h. After washing with PBS, the larvae were then imaged as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design strategy, preparation and characterization of the LD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since LDs consist of various neutral lipids, lipophilic probes are more inclined to interact with LDs. According to previous research, fluorescence probes with lipophilic substituents, such as alkyl chain and phenyl, are prone to selectively label LDs [23]. Moreover, some studies have shown that CDs can inherit some specific characteristics of precursors [33]. Furthermore, CDs typically have various surface functional groups covering their surfaces and carbon cores. For example, the electron-donating functional groups (–NH2, –OH, or –CH3) may function as the electron donor, while the electron-withdrawing functional groups (–CO, –F) may act as the electron acceptor, constituting a satisfactory donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) type structure in CDs with a powerful ICT effect [23]. The CDs with the ICT effect may improve the sensitivity of the response to polarity. Consequently, by rationally choosing the precursors, CDs possessing the capability to image LDs and determine their polarity can be achieved. In our design, 3,4-diaminofluorobenzene, 3-dimethylaminophenol (3-DMAP), and thiourea are selected as precursors to prepare LD-CDs via a Schiff base reaction in a one-pot solvothermal system. The phenolic group of the 3-DMAP molecule (Fig. S1) can be readily converted to an oxidized 3-DMAP with a quinone structure in the alkaline aqueous solution. Subsequently, the CO group of the oxidized 3-DMAP can react with the NH2 group of 3, 4-diaminofluorobenzene in the presence of thiourea in ethanol by Schiff base condensation [34]. Subsequently, the intermediate product further continues to undergo dehydration, condensation, and carbonization, eventually converting into LD-CDs. In conclusion, the designed strategy can achieve the low-cost and simple preparation of CDs possessing the capability of imaging of LDs and response to polarity.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the morphology of LD-CDs, which confirmed the sphere-like morphology of LD-CDs. TEM images of LD-CDs showed well-dispersed and uniform spherical particles with a size distribution in the range of 1.0–5.5 nm, displaying an average size of 3.03 nm (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S2). HRTEM image revealed lattice fringes of the LD-CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, consistent with the (020) plane of graphite carbon (inset in Fig. 1a) [35]. The crystal phase of the LD-CDs was also confirmed by the XRD pattern, in which the diffraction peak located at 28° was consistent with the (002) facet of graphite, indicating the successful formation of CDs (Fig. S3a). In Raman spectroscopy, both the 1602 cm−1 (G-band) and 1361 cm−1 (D-band) were detected, corresponding to the sp2-hybridized carbon and the disordered or defect carbon, respectively. The ID/IG value was 1.32, indicating a high degree of defects in LD-CDs (Fig. S3b) [36]. The surface structural composition of LD-CDs was further investigated by FT-IR spectra. The broad absorption bands in the range of 3200–3400 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of –OH and N–H, while the peak between 2800 and 3000 cm−1 was associated with the C–H stretching vibration. The peaks at 1444 cm−1 and 1353 cm−1 emanated from the stretching vibrations of CC and C–N. Moreover, the signature peak at approximately 1606 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CN [37]. Besides, the absorption peaks observed at 1248 cm−1 and 1142 cm−1 provide evidence of the existence of C–O and C–F bonds (Fig. 1b). Based on the 1H NMR spectrum of LD-CDs, several domain peaks ranging from 6.0 to 7.0 ppm were attributed to the 1H in aromatic rings (Fig. S4). In addition, the aliphatic methyl protons were also found at 2.80 ppm. Additionally, the peak at 4.73 ppm indicated residual amino groups on the surface of the LD-CDs. The 13C NMR spectrum of LD-CDs is shown in Fig. S5, with notable peaks in the 100–160 ppm range corresponding to aromatic ring carbon. The peaks about 40 ppm indicated the existence of nonconjugated aliphatic sp3 carbon atoms. The mass spectra have also revealed a significant degree of carbonization and the extent of conjugation in LD-CDs, which were characterized by a higher molecular weight and a more dispersed molecular weight distribution (Fig. S6). [23]. The functional groups on the surface of LD-CDs were also analyzed by XPS spectrum. LD-CDs consisted of C, N, O, S, and F elements with atomic percents of 57.48 %, 4.63 %, 33.26 %, 23.8 %, and 1.95 %, respectively (Fig. 1c). And the high-resolution C1s spectra displayed three distinct peaks at 284.3, 285.4, and 289.5 eV, attributed to the C–C/CC, C–N, and C–F functionalities (Fig. S7a). Similarly, the N1s spectra suggested the presence of CN (398.8 eV) and N–H (399.8 eV) (Fig. S7b). In the O1s XPS profile, OC (531.3 eV), O–C (532.1 eV), and –OH (536.2 eV) were also detected in Fig. S7c [38]. The fitted peaks at 684.5 eV in the F1s XPS spectra could be assigned to covalently bonded C–F bond (Fig. S7d). The S1s spectra at 162.6, 166.8, and 169.1 eV could be fitted into three parts linked with CS/C–S, S–S, and SO groups (Fig. S7e). The aforementioned results collectively verified that the LD-CDs are comprised of aromatic structures and covered with numerous surface functional groups, such as amino groups, hydrophobic CH3 (methyl), fluorine, and carbonyl, endowing them with sensitive polarity response and LD-targeting ability. Additionally, the ζ potential of LD-CDs was calculated to be −13.7 mV (Fig. S3c).

The UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of the LD-CDs were employed to investigate their spectroscopic features. The UV–vis spectrum of LD-CDs showed dual absorption peaks at 227 nm and 283 nm, associated with π–π∗ transitions of CC bonds in aromatic sp2 domains. In addition, a broad absorption band at 396 nm originated from the n-π∗ transition of CN/CO bonds (Fig. 1d). Fluorescence spectra of LD-CDs exhibited the optimum excitation wavelength at 446 nm with an emission center at 563 nm (Fig. 1d). Due to their large Stokes shift (127 nm), LD-CDs were able to significantly reduce interference from background noise. The LD-CDs showed excitation-dependent emissive states and displayed noticeable bright orange fluorescence in ethanol (Fig. S8a). The pH stability, salt resistance, and photostability of the LD-CDs were further measured. The fluorescence intensity change of the LD-CDs was negligible within the physiological pH range (Fig. S8b). Also, minor effects were found in the presence of different concentrations of saline solutions (Fig. S8c). Additionally, with continuous irradiation at 446 nm for 1 h, no discernible change in their fluorescence intensity was detected (Fig. S8d). Hence, the synthesized LD-CDs exhibited both exceptional optical characteristics and strong stability, which is more advantageous for cell imaging.

To verify that the prepared LD-CDs possess superior polarity performance, spectral properties of LD-CDs, including absorption and emission spectra, were investigated in solvents with different polarities (Table S1). The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of LD-CDs indicated that the absorption bands were not largely affected by solvent polarity. (Fig. S9b). On the contrary, the emission peaks of LD-CDs exhibited a 69 nm redshift (523 nm–592 nm) from 1, 4-dioxane to H2O (Fig. 1e and S9c). LD-CDs presented enhanced fluorescence under lower polarity organic solvents compared with H2O. This dissimilarity in various solvents holds great potential for wash-free imaging, as organelles may exhibit decreased polarity like that of organic solvents. Notably, the fluorescence of LD-CDs has been red-shifted from the green light area to the orange light area as solvent polarity increased (Fig. S9a). We further used CIE to explore the chromaticity coordinates of LD-CDs dispersed in different solvents. The chromaticity gradually varied from the original (0.272, 0.502) to the final (0.457, 0.488) (Fig. 1f). The detailed information of chromatic values in various solvents was collected in Table S2. Therefore, the results suggest that the LD-CDs possess strong positive solvatochromism, which is in line with our initial idea for the probe design.

Next, a mixed solution of 1, 4-dioxane/H2O with different water fractions was applied to investigate the polarity-response capability of LD-CDs (Table S3). As the percentage of water increased from 0 % to 100 %, the maximum emission peak of LD-CDs displayed a notable red shift phenomenon, accompanied by a 36-fold reduction in maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1g). However, the absorption spectra exhibited a tiny shift in various percentages of 1, 4-dioxane/H2O mixtures (Fig. S9d). Simultaneously, the fluorescence color of the solution transitioned from bright green to dark orange (inset in Fig. 1g). The fluorescence intensity of LD-CDs was correlated with the polarity parameter (Δf) from 0.0205 to 0.3213 (R2 = 0.9965) (Fig. 1h). Moreover, interference tests revealed that LD-CDs exhibited minor fluorescence responses to various biomolecules, ions (Fig. S10a), demonstrating the high specificity of the LD-CDs for polarity sensing. Also, when the viscosity increased from 0 to 99 % glycerol, only an approximately 5-fold enhancement in the maximum fluorescence intensity was detected (Fig. S10b). Hence, it can be concluded that LD-CDs are not affected by viscosity. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of LD-CDs decreased from 5.41 ns to 4.06 ns when the media was changed from 1, 4-dioxane to water (Fig. S11), indicating the time-resolved response features of LD-CDs to polarity.

In view of verifying the ability of the probe to target LDs, n-octanol, possessing a structure analogous to lipids, was employed to simulate the in vitro lipid environment [39]. Furthermore, it was noteworthy that yellow fluorescence of LD-CDs in n-octanol was much stronger (with the fluorescence intensity about 20-fold higher) than in water (Fig. 1i), demonstrating the potential of the LD-CDs as a lipid light-on probe. In addition, the oil-water partition coefficient (LogP) is extensively applied to estimate the lipophilicity of the CDs, which can be obtained by the “shake flask” approach [40]. The LD-CDs (LogP about 1.42) (Fig. S12) exhibit comparable lipophilicity to the commercial LDs probe Nile Red, signifying the substantial hydrophobicity of the LD-CDs. The quantum yield (QY) was significantly enhanced from 1.45 % (water) to 11.29 % (n-octanol). The excellent lipophilicity guarantees the strong emission of the LD-CDs in LDs for specific LDs targeting.

Prior to fluorescence imaging, we initially examined the cytotoxicity of LD-CDs by the MTT assay using HeLa cells as the model cell. The results showed that the cell viability was greater than 90 % even at concentrations of LD-CDs up to 40 μg/mL (Fig. S13), indicating that LD-CDs are suitable for bio-imaging experiments. When the concentration of LD-CDs increased, cell brightness consistently enhanced, demonstrating that more LD-CDs entered the cells. Therefore, the best concentration of CDs for imaging was chosen to be 40 μg/mL (Fig. S14). The LD-CDs could rapidly be transported into cells, and the fluorescence signal gradually reached a steady state within 2 min (Fig. S15). Subsequently, we identified LD-CDs at a concentration of 40 μg/mL and an incubation duration of 2 min as suitable for following the imaging applications. In addition, we also demonstrated that LD-CDs possessed higher photostability and specificity than Nile red (Fig. S16), which holds the great potential for long-term tracking and imaging. To verify the specificity LD-CDs for targeting LDs, organelle colocalization imaging experiments were carried out using commercial organelle-localized dyes. As shown in Fig. 2 , obvious spherical spots were observed; the yellow channel (LD-CDs) overlapped perfectly with the red channel (Nile red) with a high Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.85. Conversely, LD-CDs stained with Mito-Tracker Green, Lyso-Tracker Deep Red, and Hoechst demonstrated weak colocalization, with PC values of 0.34, 0.40, and 0.25, respectively. The co-incubation imaging results suggest that the LD-CDs possess an excellent ability to image LDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diagnosis of cancer cells by LDs-specific labeling
ce:section.ce:para: There is increasing evidence that the LDs polarity differs between normal and cancer cells, prompting us to investigate the biological feasibility of LD-CDs via specifically labeling LDs to identify cancer cells. Therefore, four types of cells, including HeLa cells, A549 cells, HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells, were selected for the assay. As anticipated, all cancer cells displayed remarkable fluorescence signal compared to healthy cells. In comparison to normal cells, a greater quantity and larger size of yellow fluorescent dots were also detected in cancer cells (Fig. S17). This observation indicates that owing to the sensitivity to increased low polarity lipids, LD-CDs could be employed to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of the dynamic behavior of LDs
ce:section.ce:para: LDs are regarded as dynamic organelles, and their movements and functions are directly linked to cellular metabolism. Therefore, we then examined the capability of the LD-CDs in real-time detection of dynamic activities of LDs. A color-coded system comprising red, green, yellow, and blue, was employed to show the activities of the LDs at different times. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the majority of fluorescent dots in the observed area rarely overlapped, presenting rapid movements of LDs. The LDs labeled with LD-CDs showed rapid movement within 2 min, involving dissociation, migration, and fusion (Fig. 3b). The occurrence of highly dynamic behaviors of LDs in cancer cells was primarily caused by the requirement to meet lipid metabolism during rapid growth. Thus, these data suggest that LD-CDs are applicable for real-time observation of LDs dynamics, which holds considerable significance for early diagnosis of LD-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the process of lipophagy between LDs and lysosomes
ce:section.ce:para: LDs and lysosomes are highly dynamic organelles rather than only being passive cellular components of cell. Lipophagy is a significant model of intracellular lipid metabolism and is closely associated with numerous diseases [41]. Encouraged by the superior biocompatibility and high staining selectivity for LDs, LD-CDs were employed to observe the lipophagy process induced by starvation. Firstly, we cultured HeLa cells with LD-CDs and LysoTracker Red under starvation conditions at varied time nodes. At the beginning of the observation, the morphology and amount of LDs and lysosomes were distinctly separated. Under continued starvation, the number of LDs gradually decreased, and the fluorescence signal originating from LDs considerably disappeared. Meanwhile, some of the red and yellow spots gradually overlapped, indicating the damaged LDs were absorbed by lysosomes through lipophagy (Fig. 4 ), which supports the formation of autolysosomes during lipophagy. The above results suggested that cells would utilize the lipids stored in LDs to maintain energy metabolism during periods of starvation [42]. To further prove this conclusion, we then incubated cells with the autophagy inhibitor, 3-MA. It was observed that these cells showed a constant fluorescence intensity for HeLa cells (Fig. S18), demonstrating that the probe LD-CDs can be used to monitor dynamic changes of LDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring LDs changes induced by oleic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Since LDs are essential for preventing fatty acid-induced lipotoxicity and lipoapoptosis [43], cells will produce a lot of them to satisfy the need for the quick conversion of free fatty acids. Oleic acid (OA), as an LDs-stimulating inducer, is widely used to induce cells to form a significant amount of LDs [44]. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, the number of intracellular LDs and the fluorescence intensity of LDs became gradually stronger when OA administration was prolonged. Consequently, it may be concluded that the increase of fatty acids facilitates the conversion and storage of lipids, resulting in the proliferation and enlargement of LDs [45]. Simultaneously, the polarity in LDs decreases owing to the accumulation of lipids, which contributes to the fluorescence increase. These findings validate LD-CDs as a valuable tool for monitoring intracellular LDs accumulation and determining their polarity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of lipid metabolism in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: As a significant in vivo experimental model, zebrafish are extensively employed to investigate lipid dynamics and lipid-associated diseases, such as fatty liver disease, obesity, and pancreatic steatosis [46]. Then, the superior LDs labeling capability of LD-CDs prompted us to expand their application in monitoring lipid metabolism in zebrafish. No mortality of zebrafish was observed during the process of imaging, further indicating the excellent biocompatibility of the LD-CDs. Thus, zebrafish embryos were labeled with LD-CDs. The intense fluorescence was found in the yolk sac of the 24-hpf zebrafish. Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity of LD-CDs in 36, 48, and 72-hpf zebrafish diminished to 79 %, 61 %, and 46 % (Fig. 6 ), respectively, indicating lipid levels declined gradually as time went on due to the absorption of the yolk. The above results provide support for the notion that LD-CDs may be useful for staining the yolk lipids. Meanwhile, the honeycomb pattern was obviously observed upon enlarging the zebrafish surface (Fig. S19), corresponding to the epidermal cell membranes.

Lastly, cheered by the above satisfactory bioimaging results, tracing the variations of LDs under oleic acid-induced formation of LDs in zebrafish larvae was then attempted. The yellow fluorescence showed an obvious enhancement with the prolonged time of OA stimulation (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the above imaging results demonstrated that LD-CDs present a promising avenue for tracking lipid transport and metabolism processes in vivo.

In conclusion, this work proposed a new design strategy for the one-step preparation of novel LDs-targeted carbon dots (LD-CDs) by Schiff base reaction. Notably, the LD-CDs showed high lipophilicity and sensitivity to polarity, rendering them excellent for observing LDs dynamic behaviors, including dissociation, migration, fusion, and lipophagy processes. Furthermore, the LD-CDs could discriminate cancer cells. More intriguingly, the LD-CDs were not only employed to track the polarity change of LDs induced by OA in living cells, but also enabled the visualization of different polarities in lipid-rich regions and lipid metabolism in zebrafish. As a result, LD-CDs hold great potential for studying the functions of substantial lipid storage and dynamics. This work also offers a new approach for designing LDs-targeting CDs, facilitating the development of functional CDs and their utilization in fluorescence imaging.

Yachu Meng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jianhua Guo: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Hongmei Xu: Supervision, Investigation. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Resources, Investigation. Chuan Dong: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.22274090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2025.127625.
